questions.question: Which HTML5 element is specifically designed to contain a set of navigation links?
questions.explanation: The <nav> element is the semantic HTML5 element for navigation. It should contain major navigation blocks such as site-wide navigation, table of contents, or breadcrumbs.

questions.question: What ARIA role is implicitly associated with the <nav> element?
questions.explanation: The <nav> element has an implicit ARIA role of 'navigation'. This means screen readers automatically recognize it as a navigation landmark without needing an explicit role attribute.

questions.question: Which of the following is a best practice when creating navigation bars for accessibility?
choices: Use <div> elements with onclick handlers instead of links
Use an unordered list (<ul>) of links inside a <nav> element
Avoid using any ARIA attributes on navigation elements
Use only images as navigation items without alt text
questions.explanation: Using a <ul> with <li> elements containing <a> links inside a <nav> element is a widely recommended pattern. It provides semantic structure, is keyboard-navigable by default, and is well-supported by screen readers.

questions.question: When a page has multiple <nav> elements, what should be added to help screen reader users distinguish between them?
choices: A unique id attribute on each <nav>
An aria-label or aria-labelledby attribute on each <nav>
questions.explanation: When multiple <nav> elements exist on a page, each should have an aria-label (e.g., aria-label="Main navigation") or aria-labelledby attribute so screen reader users can distinguish between them.

questions.question: The aria-___ attribute provides a text label for a navigation element when no visible heading is present.
questions.explanation: The aria-label attribute provides an accessible name for an element when no visible text label exists. For navigation, it helps screen reader users understand the purpose of the nav (e.g., aria-label="Main navigation").

questions.question: The currently active page in a navigation bar should be indicated with the aria-___="page" attribute.
questions.explanation: The aria-current="page" attribute indicates the current page within a set of navigation links. Screen readers announce this to users so they know which page they are currently on.
